items.name: Bridge
items.short: Supports the strings and transmits vibrations to the body.
items.long: The bridge is bought as a rough-cut maple blank and fitted individually to each instrument. The luthier carves the feet to match the exact arch of the top plate, sets the height for correct string action, and refines the curves — a process that takes 1 to 2 hours per bridge.

byIntent.function: The bridge holds the strings at the correct height above the fingerboard and transmits their vibration into the body of the violin. It is never glued — string tension alone holds it upright, which is why a bridge can shift or fall if a string breaks.
byIntent.sound: Because the bridge is the only physical link between the strings and the body, its wood density directly shapes the tone. Heavier maple vibrates more slowly, which luthiers use to help eliminate unwanted wolf tones; a lighter bridge tends to brighten the sound.
byIntent.history: Bridges have kept roughly the same silhouette since the violin's earliest form in 16th-century Cremona, though the curve and cutout pattern have been refined for centuries to balance strength with acoustic transparency.
byIntent.compare: Unlike the sound post, which sits hidden inside the body, the bridge is the only major part of a violin that is entirely external and fully visible — and the only one not glued in place.

items.name: F-holes
items.short: Sound holes that let air move in and out of the body.
items.long: The f-holes are the two f-shaped openings cut into the belly of the violin. They allow air to move freely in and out of the body, which is essential for projection and tonal richness.

byIntent.function: F-holes let the luthier reach inside the body during construction and repair — small clamps can be inserted through them to reglue open seams from the inside without taking the instrument apart.
byIntent.sound: The f-holes act as the violin's acoustic vent, allowing the air resonance inside the body to escape and project outward. Their length and width are tuned almost as precisely as the plates themselves — a fraction of a millimetre changes the instrument's air resonance frequency.
byIntent.history: The f-shape replaced simpler round or C-shaped sound holes used on early bowed instruments. By Stradivari's time the f-hole had been refined into the elegant, elongated form still used today.
byIntent.compare: Where the scroll is purely decorative, the f-holes are purely functional — removing them would silence the instrument almost entirely, even though they take up a small fraction of the surface area.

items.name: Scroll
items.short: The decorative spiral at the top — the maker's artistic signature.
items.long: The scroll is carved by hand using gouges and a Japanese restoration saw for the initial rough cut, followed by finer work with chisels.

byIntent.function: The scroll caps the pegbox at the top of the neck and serves as a handhold when the instrument is moved or held — beyond that, it plays no role in producing sound.
byIntent.sound: The scroll has essentially no acoustic function. Its mass is too far from the vibrating body and strings to meaningfully affect tone, which is why makers feel free to carve it expressively rather than to any acoustic specification.
byIntent.history: Historically the scroll is the maker's artistic signature — expert luthiers can often identify who made a violin just by examining the scroll's proportions and carving style, the way an art historian recognises a painter's brushwork.
byIntent.compare: Unlike the f-holes or sound post, which are governed by acoustic necessity, the scroll is the one part of the violin where the maker has almost complete creative freedom.

items.name: Finger Plane
items.short: A miniature hand plane for shaping the arched plates.
items.long: The finger plane — also called a thumb plane — is a tiny hand plane held between the fingers, used for fine surface finishing on the top and back plates.

byIntent.function: Held between the fingers rather than gripped like a standard plane, it shaves thin curls of wood to refine the arching of the top and back plates after the rougher carving is done.
byIntent.sound: The finger plane doesn't touch the final sound directly, but it determines the precise graduation — the thickness profile — of the plates, which is one of the single biggest factors in how a violin resonates.
byIntent.history: Specialist hand planes like this have been used by Cremonese luthiers since the Amati and Stradivari workshops of the 16th and 17th centuries, with the basic tool shape barely changed in 400 years.
byIntent.compare: The finger plane and the scraper do similar work in sequence — the plane removes more material quickly, while the scraper that follows it produces the final glass-smooth surface.

items.name: Scraper
items.short: A steel blade that produces a glass-smooth surface.
items.long: The scraper is a thin flat piece of hardened steel used after the finger plane to shave and smooth wood surfaces.

byIntent.function: Scrapers come in several shapes for different parts — a large flat one for the belly and back, curved ones for arched surfaces, a tightly curved one for the fingerboard radius, and a straight rigid one for the ribs.
byIntent.sound: A properly finished surface from the scraper affects how evenly varnish absorbs into the wood later, which in turn affects the tonal consistency across the instrument's surface.
byIntent.history: Sharpening angle has been debated among luthiers for generations: a 45° edge cuts more aggressively, while 90° is gentler — different workshops favour different angles as part of their individual technique.
byIntent.compare: Where the finger plane removes material, the scraper refines it — a properly sharpened scraper produces fine curling shavings rather than dust, the opposite of how sandpaper works.

items.name: Wood & Varnish
items.short: The legendary Cremonese varnish — ground, UV, pigment, oil.
items.long: Jönke described the varnishing process in detail: a ground layer is applied to the bare wood, then placed under a UV lamp for several days to give the wood its characteristic golden base.

byIntent.function: Varnish protects the wood from moisture and wear, and helps the surface withstand decades of handling — but in Cremonese tradition it's applied as thinly as possible so it doesn't dampen the wood's natural vibration.
byIntent.sound: Coloured oil varnish is applied in thin layers over the UV-cured ground; too thick a coat can noticeably mute the instrument's resonance, which is why luthiers build it up gradually rather than in one heavy coat.
byIntent.history: Jönke mixes every shade — including the deep red-amber associated with classic Cremonese instruments — from just four pigments sourced from Mr. Dimes in England. The exact formula is a closely guarded secret, passed down workshop by workshop; as he put it, 'Amati would kill me.'
byIntent.compare: Unlike touch-up paint, which is mixed to match an existing finish during restoration, the original varnish is built from scratch in careful layers on bare, UV-treated wood.

items.name: Tuning Fork
items.short: Produces a perfect reference pitch for tuning.
items.long: The tuning fork is a two-pronged metal instrument that vibrates at a precise frequency when struck, producing a pure reference tone.

byIntent.function: Luthiers strike it and hold the base against a surface to use the reference pitch — typically A440 — as the starting point from which every string on the violin is tuned.
byIntent.sound: Because a tuning fork's frequency is fixed by its physical dimensions rather than tension like a string, it gives an extremely stable reference tone that doesn't drift with temperature the way a violin string can.
byIntent.history: The tuning fork was invented in 1711 by English musician John Shore, making it newer than the violin itself, which had already been refined in Cremona for over a century by then.
byIntent.compare: Unlike every other tool in the workshop, the tuning fork shapes nothing — it exists purely as a reference, the opposite role to cutting or carving tools like the scraper or finger plane.

items.name: Tuning Peg
items.short: Adjusts string tension to tune the violin.
items.long: Tuning pegs are tapered wooden pins fitted into the pegbox, traditionally made from ebony, rosewood, or boxwood.

byIntent.function: Turning a peg winds or unwinds the string, increasing or decreasing tension to raise or lower its pitch — friction between the tapered peg and the pegbox hole is what holds it in place once set.
byIntent.sound: Peg wood choice affects vibration at the scroll end of the instrument — different densities change how that end of the violin resonates, contributing subtly to the overall tone.
byIntent.history: Friction pegs of this same basic tapered design have been used on bowed string instruments since long before the modern violin existed in 16th-century Cremona, making them one of the oldest unchanged components.
byIntent.compare: While the tailpiece anchors strings at the bottom, the tuning pegs anchor and adjust them at the top — together the two ends determine the full vibrating length and tension of each string.

items.name: Wood File
items.short: Shapes and smooths wood during construction.
items.long: Wood files are essential luthier tools used to shape, fit, and smooth wooden parts of the violin, available in flat, round, and half-round profiles.

byIntent.function: Different file profiles let the maker work both flat surfaces and curved areas, such as the inside of the scroll or the edges of the plates, where a plane or scraper can't easily reach.
byIntent.sound: Files are typically used on structural and decorative areas like the scroll and edges rather than the resonating plates themselves, so their impact on sound is mostly indirect — through overall fit and finish quality.
byIntent.history: Tools like Jönke's were bought used and slightly rusty — in his workshop, patina on a tool is treated as irrelevant to performance, a practical attitude shared by many working luthiers rather than collectors.
byIntent.compare: Where the finger plane and scraper are used mainly on the broad flat and arched plate surfaces, the wood file is reached for when working tighter curves and edges those tools can't access.

items.name: UV Cabin
items.short: Accelerates varnish curing using ultraviolet light.
items.long: Jönke uses a UV lamp to accelerate what sunlight would do naturally over months, producing the golden yellowing of the wood ground in just a few days.

byIntent.function: The instrument's ground layer is placed inside the UV cabin for several days; the cabin is switched off whenever anyone is nearby, since the light is harmful to the eyes.
byIntent.sound: The UV-cured ground forms the base that the coloured varnish sits on top of — a more even, consistent cure here helps the later varnish layers build up smoothly without affecting how freely the wood can vibrate.
byIntent.history: This is one of the few genuinely modern tools in an otherwise centuries-old process — historically, makers like Stradivari relied entirely on natural sunlight over months to achieve the same golden base tone.
byIntent.compare: Unlike touch-up paint, which is mixed to repair existing finishes, the UV cabin works only on bare new wood, right at the very start of the varnishing process.

items.name: Touch Up Paint
items.short: Used to restore and repair varnish on old violins.
items.long: Touch up paint is a carefully mixed varnish used by restorers to repair scratches, chips, and worn areas on antique violins.

byIntent.function: A restorer applies it precisely to damaged spots — scratches, chips, worn patches — to repair the finish without needing to revarnish the entire instrument.
byIntent.sound: Because it's applied only to small, localised areas, touch-up paint has negligible effect on tone — its job is purely cosmetic and protective, unlike the original varnish coats applied during construction.
byIntent.history: Matching the exact colour and texture of centuries-old Cremonese varnish is considered one of the most delicate skills in instrument restoration, since old varnish ages and shifts in tone over decades.
byIntent.compare: Jönke mixes touch-up colours from the same four base pigments used for original varnish, controlling transparency by pigment ratio — more pigment for opacity, less to let the wood grain show through.

items.name: Ribs
items.short: Thin maple strips bent to form the sides of the body.
items.long: The ribs are thin strips of maple bent into shape using a bending iron heated to 160–180°C, then assembled around an internal mold.

byIntent.function: The wood is moistened slightly, pressed against the hot iron with gentle pressure for 10 to 20 seconds, then fanned to cool — the heat breaks down lignin in the wood fibres, letting them reset permanently in the new curved shape.
byIntent.sound: The ribs form the side walls of the resonating body and determine its depth, which influences the air volume inside and therefore the instrument's overall resonant character.
byIntent.history: Bending wood with a heated iron to shape violin ribs is a technique that has remained essentially unchanged since the earliest Cremonese workshops of the 1500s.
byIntent.compare: While the violin back is carved from a solid block of figured maple, the ribs are made from much thinner strips and shaped entirely by bending rather than carving.

items.name: Sound Post
items.short: A spruce rod inside the body that transfers vibrations.
items.long: The sound post is a small cylindrical spruce rod wedged inside the body between the top and back plates, positioned just behind the treble foot of the bridge.

byIntent.function: It is inserted and positioned using a thin hooked rod passed through the f-hole, since it isn't glued in place — only friction and precise fit hold it upright.
byIntent.sound: It transmits vibration from the top plate to the back plate and provides structural support against string pressure. Moving it even slightly changes the sound: closer to the bridge gives a brighter, tighter tone, further away gives more warmth and openness.
byIntent.history: Luthiers have nicknamed it 'the soul' of the violin in several European languages (anima in Italian, âme in French) for centuries, reflecting just how disproportionately important this small, hidden part is to the instrument's character.
byIntent.compare: Like the bridge, the sound post is never glued — both rely entirely on string tension and precise fit, making them the two most adjustable parts of an otherwise fixed instrument.

items.name: Tailpiece
items.short: Anchors the strings at the bottom of the violin.
items.long: The tailpiece anchors the lower ends of all four strings and is attached to the body via the tailgut looped around the end pin.

byIntent.function: Modern tailpieces often include built-in fine tuners, small screws that allow for precise pitch adjustment without having to turn the larger pegs at the top of the instrument.
byIntent.sound: The weight and density of the tailpiece influences how the instrument resonates — Jönke discussed how components at both ends of the string, pegs and tailpiece alike, affect overall vibrational behaviour.
byIntent.history: Early tailpieces were carved from a single piece of wood with no fine tuners at all; fine tuners became widespread only as steel strings — which need much finer pitch control than gut strings — grew popular in the 20th century.
byIntent.compare: Where the tuning pegs handle large pitch changes at the top of the string, the tailpiece's fine tuners (when fitted) handle small precise adjustments at the bottom.

items.name: Violin Back
items.short: The back plate, carved from figured maple.
items.long: The back plate is typically carved from figured maple sourced from Romania and the Balkans, currently considered among the best regions for violin-quality wood.

byIntent.function: Jönke visits specialist warehouses, inspects individual pieces for figure (the flame pattern), density, and resonance, and selects the wood by feel and tap tone before any carving begins.
byIntent.sound: The back plate reflects vibration from the sound post back through the body, working in tandem with the spruce top — the maple's density and stiffness shape how brightly or warmly the instrument projects.
byIntent.history: A violin takes 200 to 250 hours to make in total, and selecting the right piece of maple for the back is considered half the job — the wood's quality sets a ceiling on the instrument's eventual sound that no amount of skilled carving can exceed.
byIntent.compare: While the top plate is carved from spruce for lightness and flexibility, the back is carved from denser maple — the pairing of the two different woods is fundamental to how a violin produces its characteristic tone.

items.name: Violin Mold
items.short: The internal form used to shape the violin body during construction.
items.long: The violin mold is a wooden template around which the ribs are bent and assembled to create the correct body shape.

byIntent.function: The ribs are glued and clamped around the mold while still warm and pliable from bending; once the rib structure is set, the mold is removed and the body is closed with the top and back plates.
byIntent.sound: The mold doesn't touch the finished instrument at all and has no acoustic role itself — but the precision of its shape determines the body outline and proportions that do define the violin's resonance.
byIntent.history: Jönke uses internal molds, the same construction approach used by the Cremonese masters. Many of Stradivari's original molds are preserved at the Museo del Violino in Cremona, giving modern luthiers a direct physical link to his historical methods.
byIntent.compare: Unlike every other item in this workshop, the mold never becomes part of the finished violin at all — it exists purely as a temporary construction aid, then is removed and reused for the next instrument.
